key_strengths: Duopoly with IAA controlling ~80% of US salvage auctions, protected by 250+ owned yards with decades of zoning/permitting barriers
Fortress balance sheet: $5.1B net cash, zero meaningful debt, 8.4x current ratio, positive FCF through any plausible stress scenario
Adjusted ROIC on operating assets of 29-32%, with $1.6B in owner earnings on an asset-light consignment model
Founder Willis Johnson holds ~$2B in stock; CEO equity locked up 10 years; SBC at 0.8% of revenue among lowest in corporate America
Secular tailwind from rising total loss frequency (15.6% in 2015 to 24.2% in 2025) driven by increasing vehicle complexity
key_risks: DOJ money laundering investigation with unquantifiable financial exposure and potential operational restrictions on international buyer access
Q2 FY2026 revenue declined 3.6% YoY with operating margin compressing from 36.6% to 34.7% — near-term cyclical headwinds
IAA/RB Global competitive resurgence: unit growth +4.7% vs Copart's -2.8%, Progressive Insurance shifted volume toward IAA
Capital allocation inefficiency: $4.8B cash pile earning sub-equity returns with no dividend and modest buybacks
Growth capex at $350M+/year in land acquisition with no explicit ROI disclosure; international expansion unproven at scale
red_flags: DOJ money laundering investigation — unknown scope and timeline (1-3 years to resolution)
First meaningful revenue decline in years (Q2 FY2026 -3.6% YoY)
Net insider selling of 1.6M shares over 18 months with zero open-market purchases at near-52-week-low prices
Headline ROIC declining four consecutive years (24.2% to 16.6%)
buy_triggers: Q3/Q4 FY2026 earnings showing revenue growth resumption and operating margin stabilization
DOJ investigation resolution or settlement with manageable financial terms and no structural restrictions on international buyer access
Stock price declining to $25-27 range providing genuine price MOS against bear case IV
sell_triggers: DOJ outcome that restricts international buyer participation, structurally impairing network effect and ASP premium
ROIC on operating assets (excluding excess cash) falling below 15% for two consecutive years
Loss of a top-3 insurance customer to IAA, indicating switching cost moat is weaker than assessed
valuation_summary: At $32.87, CPRT trades at 21x trailing earnings vs a 5-year average of 35x — a 40% multiple compression. The quant model base IV of $34.33 is 4.4% above current price. Analyst-adjusted estimates: bear $23, base $36, bull $50. The market is pricing in 6% perpetual growth, roughly half Copart's 10% historical CAGR. Monte Carlo shows 77% probability of being undervalued with median IV at $35.82. Owner earnings of $1.6B imply a 19.5x P/OE multiple — reasonable for a capital-light duopoly compounder.
compact_checklist: Copart runs online auctions for totaled vehicles on behalf of insurance companies, earning fees on both sides of a transaction that recurs because cars will always crash and modern vehicles are increasingly too expensive to repair.

A duopoly with IAA controlling ~80% of US salvage auctions, protected by 250+ owned yards that take decades of zoning and permitting to replicate, plus network effects where 1M+ global buyers drive ASPs 3x higher than competitors.

Adjusted ROIC on operating assets is 29-32% (headline 16.6% is depressed by $4.8B idle cash), the consignment model requires no inventory risk, and owner earnings hit $1.6B in FY2025 on 45% gross margins.

Founder Willis Johnson holds ~$2B in stock as Chairman, CEO Liaw's equity is locked up for 10 years, and SBC at 0.8% of revenue is among the lowest in corporate America — though the $4.8B cash pile earns below cost of equity.
With $5.1B net cash, zero meaningful debt, and an 8.4x current ratio, Copart would generate positive free cash flow even through a 30% revenue decline sustained for two years.

At 21x trailing earnings versus a 5-year average of 35x, the market is pricing in 6% perpetual growth — roughly half Copart's 10% historical revenue CAGR — meaning you need only base-case execution for the stock to work.
The business cannot go to zero (duopoly, $5.1B cash, $1.2B annual FCF), but there is no price MOS against the bear case IV of $23, so the protection is business quality rather than cheap entry.

Sell if the DOJ investigation results in structural restrictions on international buyer access, or if ROIC on operating assets falls below 15% for two consecutive years signaling the duopoly's pricing power is broken.
source_summary: Copart FY2025 10-K, Q1/Q2 FY2026 10-Q filings, Yahoo Finance (yfinance auto-fetch), quant-valuation DCF model, Seeking Alpha, Simply Wall St, GuruFocus, StockAnalysis.com, Motley Fool, DOJ disclosure filings, web search for competitive landscape and industry analysis.
